message:
perf(btree): quotient filter for fast absent-key DELETE/UPDATE reject

Per Bender et al., SEA 2012 "Don't Thrash: How to Cache Your Hash on Flash"
(https://www3.cs.stonybrook.edu/~bender/newpub/BenderFaJo12-sea.pdf), add a
probabilistic membership filter module supporting insert / contains / remove
so callers on the DELETE FROM t WHERE rowid=? and UPDATE t SET ... WHERE
rowid=? hot paths can fast-reject rowids that were never present without
doing a full B-tree descent.

Module: crates/fsqlite-btree/src/quotient_filter.rs

Semantics guaranteed:
* False-negative rate = 0 (any insert(h)ed key always reports contains(h)
  until remove(h)).
* False-positive rate configurable via (q_bits, r_bits). Defaults: q=20,
  r=16 → 1 Mi-slot filter with theoretical FP ≈ load / 2^16 ≈ 1.5e-5 at
  50% load. `with_capacity(expected_entries, target_load, r_bits)` sizes
  the table to the next power of two ≥ expected_entries / target_load.

Sizing:
* q=20 (default) produces 2^20 ≈ 1 Mi slots, ~24 MiB resident for 1 Mi
  logical entries. For a 10K-row table with_capacity picks q=15 (32768
  slots, r=14).

FP-rate measurement (see quotient_filter::tests):
* fp_rate_within_theoretical_bound: 8192 inserts into a (q=14, r=10)
  filter, 100k disjoint probes. Observed FP ≤ 6x theoretical bound.
* workload_10k_absent_keys_mostly_rejected: 10k inserts + 10k disjoint
  probes with r=14. Observed FP ≤ 10/10000 (loose ceiling; typical runs
  see 0-2 false positives).

Implementation note: the on-disk layout in Bender's paper packs three
metadata bits (`is_occupied`, `is_continuation`, `is_shifted`) + r
remainder bits per slot for a dense ≈ r+3 bits/entry encoding. That
encoding is notoriously subtle to maintain correctly across random
insert/remove workloads. This commit ships a correctness-first variant
that keeps the same externally-observable contract (insert, contains,
remove with deterministic FN=0 and probabilistic FP bounded by
load / 2^r) using a per-slot inline-bucket layout — ~24 MiB per 1 Mi-
slot filter instead of ~8 MiB — and documents the tradeoff. The
canonical Bender encoding can be retrofitted later under the same
public API without touching callers.

Benchmark (cargo test -p fsqlite-btree --release --lib quotient_filter_bench
-- --ignored --nocapture) for the prompt's 10K-row / 10K-absent-probe
scenario:

    rows               = 10000
    absent probes      = 10000

    QF contains        :   63.329µs total,      6.3 ns/op
    Sorted Vec bsearch :  123.652µs total,     12.4 ns/op
    Linear Vec scan   : 38.192143ms total,   3819.2 ns/op

    QF is 1.95x faster than sorted Vec binary_search
    QF is 603.08x faster than linear Vec scan

PRAGMA hook is deliberately deferred: the Connection / VDBE wiring that
would intercept DELETE/UPDATE WHERE rowid=? before btree descent is
intentionally out of scope for this commit to avoid churn across
crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs (5.4 MiB, many agents active). The
filter is a self-contained, well-tested primitive ready for a follow-up
commit that threads it through Connection state under a
`PRAGMA fsqlite.absent_key_filter = on|off` switch defaulting off.

Validation:
* cargo test -p fsqlite-btree --lib quotient_filter : 19 tests pass
  (constructor, FP bound, adversarial same-canonical, churn, 10k workload,
  zero-FN-under-churn, collision tolerance, with_capacity sizing).
* cargo clippy -p fsqlite-btree --all-targets -- -D warnings : clean.
* cargo fmt --check -p fsqlite-btree : clean.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
